items.title: White House issues executive order on advanced AI
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 2 addressing AI innovation, security, and cyber defense for frontier AI models.
items.details: The order directs federal agencies to accelerate AI-enabled cybersecurity initiatives, establish a voluntary framework for engagement with developers of frontier AI models before broader release, and prioritize criminal enforcement against AI-enabled cyberattacks. It sets timelines of 30 days for cybersecurity directives and 60 days for the frontier model framework.
items.source: Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP

items.title: Executive order directs customs enforcement reform
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 3 directing the Department of Homeland Security and Customs and Border Protection to strengthen customs enforcement.
items.details: The order directs DHS and CBP to increase bonding requirements for importers of record, establish disclosure and certification requirements, and set a 50% minimum penalty floor on importers who violate customs laws. It also directs DHS to publish annual transparency reports and propose legislation.

items.title: Executive order reclassifies federal workforce positions
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 3 reclassifying about 8,000 senior policy-influencing federal positions into Schedule Policy/Career.
items.details: The order states that these positions remain career roles with non-partisan hiring processes unchanged, and that removal decisions will be made without respect to political affiliation. The reclassified positions are designated at-will, allowing removal for poor performance, misconduct, or other stated grounds.

items.title: U.S. and Iran reach interim agreement to extend ceasefire
items.summary: Mediators announced on June 14 a memorandum of understanding intended to formally end the 2026 Iran war within 60 days, with a signing ceremony expected.
items.details: The United States and Iran reached an initial agreement that would extend their ceasefire and lead to the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. The memorandum was signed electronically by both sides, with a formal signing ceremony expected in Geneva. The deal leaves Iran's nuclear program to later negotiations.

items.title: Iran links deal to Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon
items.summary: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said on June 16 that the agreement with the U.S. requires Israeli forces to leave Lebanon.
items.details: Araghchi said any future Israeli military attack on Lebanon or continued occupation of Lebanese territory would be regarded as a violation of the memorandum of understanding. Israeli officials have said they do not plan to withdraw troops from southern Lebanon. Iran's Revolutionary Guard said the country would receive part of roughly $24 billion in frozen funds.

items.title: U.S. launched strikes in Iran following downed helicopter
items.summary: The U.S. military said it launched strikes against multiple targets in Iran on June 9 in response to Iran downing a U.S. helicopter.
items.details: President Trump warned the U.S. could resume attacks, citing slow progress in negotiations. He also said he had directed the military to conduct a mission supporting commercial ships through the Strait of Hormuz, stating more than 200 commercial vessels traversed the waterway.

items.title: Senate rejects motion on Iran war powers resolution
items.summary: On June 16, the Senate rejected a motion to discharge a joint resolution directing removal of U.S. armed forces from hostilities with Iran not authorized by Congress.
items.details: The vote on S.J.Res. 172 was 47 in favor and 48 against, with five senators not voting. The resolution sought to direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.

items.title: FISA Section 702 authorization lapses amid funding actions
items.summary: GovTrack reported that the week of June 8 saw anti-fraud bills pass and DHS funding extended through 2029, while FISA's Section 702 authorization lapsed.
items.details: According to the legislative recap, many anti-fraud bills passed mostly by voice vote and DHS received additional funding through 2029. The Section 702 surveillance authorization lapsed, though the program itself is described as likely to continue uninterrupted.

items.title: Senate considers nominations including ambassador posts
items.summary: The Senate scheduled a cloture vote for June 17 on the nomination of George Holding to be U.S. Director of the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development.
items.details: The Senate began consideration of the nomination and a cloture vote was set to occur after disposition of the nomination of Michelle Steel to be Ambassador to South Korea. The Commerce Committee also scheduled a June 18 business meeting on legislation related to name, image, and likeness rights.

items.title: Supreme Court allows Alabama to use contested congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court on June 2 cleared the way for Alabama to use a congressional map in 2026 that lower courts found racially discriminatory.
items.details: The Court issued an unsigned order permitting Alabama to hold its 2026 elections using the map. Justice Sotomayor dissented, joined by Justices Kagan and Jackson. The case had been sent back to lower courts in light of the Court's earlier decision in Louisiana v. Callais.

items.title: Supreme Court has multiple major cases pending before recess
items.summary: U.S. News reported the Supreme Court still had several major cases to decide as it approached its summer recess.
items.details: Pending cases include challenges related to birthright citizenship, campaign finance limits, mail-in ballots, and Second Amendment issues. Several decisions are expected to affect state laws and election-related processes ahead of the midterm elections, with rulings expected by late June or early July.

items.title: Three election law cases pending at Supreme Court
items.summary: The Congressional Research Service detailed three election law cases pending at the Supreme Court that could affect the 2026 elections.
items.details: The cases are Louisiana v. Callais on majority-minority districts, NRSC v. FEC on limits on coordinated party expenditures, and Watson v. RNC on counting mail-in ballots received after Election Day. The Court is expected to issue rulings by the end of June or early July.

items.title: Supreme Court lets California use new congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for California to use its newly redrawn congressional map, which shifts five seats toward Democrats, in the 2026 midterms.
items.details: The Court declined to take up an emergency appeal from California Republicans, who, joined by the Department of Justice, had challenged the map as a racial gerrymander. The map followed voter approval of Proposition 50. The justices' decision included no dissents or explanation.

items.title: Maine Senate primary sets up Collins-Platner race
items.summary: Democrat Graham Platner won Maine's June 9 Democratic Senate primary, setting up a general election against Republican Senator Susan Collins.
items.details: Platner defeated Governor Janet Mills weeks after she ended her campaign. Collins, first elected in 1996, ran unopposed for the Republican nomination as she seeks a sixth term. Democrats hold 47 Senate seats and need a net gain of four for a majority.

items.title: Mid-decade redistricting continues across multiple states
items.summary: As of early 2026, several states had adopted new congressional maps for the midterms, with litigation and additional efforts ongoing.
items.details: California, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio, and Texas passed new congressional maps between the 2024 and 2026 elections, and litigation led to a new map in Utah. Georgia, Louisiana, and New York had maps subject to ongoing litigation, while Florida, Maryland, and Virginia took action toward redistricting.

CA.title: Newsom signs election protection law
CA.summary: Governor Newsom signed a law on May 27 strengthening protections against interference and intimidation at California polling places ahead of the June 2 primary.
CA.details: Senate Bill 73 empowers the secretary of state and attorney general to veto authorization of law enforcement at polling places, prohibits warrantless search or seizure of voting machines and voter rolls, and allows officials to seek injunctive relief and civil penalties. An urgency clause allowed it to take effect before the June 2 primary.

IA.title: Reynolds signs final bills of 2026 session
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills of the 2026 legislative session on June 2, including a measure limiting gubernatorial emergency powers.
IA.details: House File 2694 bars a governor from restricting private businesses' operations or ordering places of worship to close during disasters and public health emergencies, and requires legislative approval to change election laws during emergencies. These were Reynolds' final bills as governor, as she is not seeking another term.

NH.title: Ayotte signs 18 bills into law
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 18 bills into law during the week of June 12, covering school districts, elections, and permitting.
NH.details: The bills included HB 656 on local school districts accepting federal grants, HB 1381 extending the party filing period, and HB 1425 on an online wetlands permit processing system. Others addressed decentralized autonomous organizations and financing matters.

CT.title: Lamont signs youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Governor Ned Lamont signed legislation on June 2 establishing youth online safety protections, AI regulations, and workforce training initiatives.
CT.details: Public Act 26-15 was developed by Governor Lamont, Attorney General William Tong, and State Senator James Maroney. It addresses concerns including youth social media use and artificial intelligence, and establishes workforce training initiatives for technology jobs.

NC.title: North Carolina legislature overrides veto on education tax credit
NC.summary: The North Carolina General Assembly overrode the governor's veto on June 3 of a bill opting the state into a federal education tax credit scholarship program.
NC.details: The override opted North Carolina into the federal tax credit scholarship program. As of June 4, 31 states had formally opted in, passed legislation to opt in, or indicated intent to participate, while the governors of Hawaii, New Mexico, and Oregon said they would not participate.

SC.title: South Carolina special session on redistricting and special election
SC.summary: Governor McMaster called a redistricting special session in mid-May, and a special election filled the last legislative vacancy ahead of the June 13 session.
SC.details: McMaster issued an executive order calling the General Assembly back to address the state budget and congressional districts. A proposed map backed by House Republicans could position the party to win all seven U.S. House seats. Republican Greg Ford won a Dorchester County state House special election by 21 votes after a recount.

TN.title: Tennessee enacts new congressional map
TN.summary: Tennessee legislators passed and Governor Bill Lee approved a new congressional map redrawing the Memphis-based 9th district.
TN.details: Governor Lee convened a special session following the U.S. Supreme Court's decision in Louisiana v. Callais. The new map redraws the 9th congressional district, the state's only Democratic-held and majority-Black district. Tennessee's congressional primary is scheduled for August 6.

VA.title: Virginia redistricting referendum nullified by state court
VA.summary: Virginia voters approved a redistricting referendum in April, but the state supreme court nullified the referendum and new map on May 8.
VA.details: The 2026 Virginia redistricting referendum appeared on the April 21 ballot as a legislatively referred constitutional amendment and passed by a slim margin. It was subsequently struck down by the Supreme Court of Virginia. The amendment would have allowed mid-decade redistricting of congressional districts.

UT.title: Utah to use court-adopted congressional map
UT.summary: A Utah district court judge adopted a new congressional map in November 2025 that shifts one district toward Democrats for the 2026 elections.
UT.details: Judge Dianna Gibson rejected a map submitted by the Utah Legislature and adopted a plaintiffs' proposal after finding lawmakers had undermined an independent redistricting commission established by a 2018 ballot measure. The new Salt Lake City district is approximately 43% Republican; all four current U.S. House members are Republicans.

FL.title: DeSantis signs education and union legislation
FL.summary: Governor Ron DeSantis signed SB 1296 and HB 1279 addressing teacher unions, educator pay, and education policy.
FL.details: SB 1296 provides for the decertification of school unions and fast-tracks certain salary increases. HB 1279 allows districts to provide pay incentives to high-performing teachers in lower-performing schools without collective bargaining and addresses parental rights in special education.
FL.source: Executive Office of the Governor of Florida

WI.title: Wisconsin special sessions end without action
WI.summary: Two special sessions of the Wisconsin Legislature called by the governor ended on May 14 without resolution.
WI.details: One special session, beginning April 14, was to consider a constitutional amendment to ban partisan redistricting; the other, beginning May 13, was to consider spending part of the state's budget surplus. Both ended without resolution.

KS.title: Kansas to vote on supreme court selection amendment
KS.summary: A legislatively referred constitutional amendment on selecting Kansas Supreme Court justices will appear on the August 4 ballot.
KS.details: The amendment would create partisan elections and six-year terms for justices, replacing the current system in which justices are appointed by the governor through a nominating commission. It passed the legislature with Republican support.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources for civic-education purposes and does not endorse any party, candidate, or position.
